subject: Science
overall_learning_objectives: Explain chemical properties of metals with balanced chemical equations
Understand the reactivity series and its application in displacement reactions
Describe the formation and properties of ionic compounds
Understand the basic principles of metallurgy (extraction of metals)
Explain the process of electrolytic refining
Analyze the causes and prevention of corrosion through various methods

lesson_plan.title: Chemical Properties: Reaction with Air and Water
topics_covered: Reaction of metals with Oxygen (Amphoteric oxides)
Reaction of metals with Water (Cold, Hot, and Steam)
teaching_strategy: Demonstration of $Al_{2}O_{3}$ reacting with both acid and base
Flowchart showing metal reactivity with different forms of water
Writing balanced equations for $Na, Ca, Mg, Fe$ with water
student_activities: Classify a list of oxides as Basic or Amphoteric
Predict the products when steam is passed over red-hot iron

lesson_plan.title: Reactions with Acids and Reactivity Series
topics_covered: Displacement reactions and the Reactivity Series
teaching_strategy: Explaining why $HNO_{3}$ is a strong oxidizing agent
Lab demonstration: Zinc reacting with $CuSO_{4}$
Deriving the reactivity order from experimental data
student_activities: Determine if a reaction will occur based on the Reactivity Series

lesson_plan.title: Formation of Ionic Compounds
teaching_strategy: Step-by-step drawing of electron transfer on the board
Analogy: Giver (Metal) and Taker (Non-metal)
Visualizing the formation of ions
student_activities: Identify the cation and anion in given compounds

lesson_plan.title: Properties of Ionic Compounds
topics_covered: Physical nature, Melting and Boiling points
teaching_strategy: Comparing melting points of salt vs. wax
Conductivity test in solid state vs. aqueous solution
Logical reasoning session: 'Why do ionic compounds have high MP/BP?'
student_activities: Summarize the 4 main properties of ionic compounds in a table
Conduct a simple solubility test with salt and kerosene

lesson_plan.title: Occurrence and Extraction of Metals
topics_covered: Extraction of metals low and middle in reactivity (Roasting and Calcination)
teaching_strategy: Flowchart of metallurgical steps
Visual comparison: Roasting (Sulphide ores) vs. Calcination (Carbonate ores)
Video on the Thermite process for railway tracks
student_activities: Differentiate between Roasting and Calcination in a T-chart
Write the chemical equation for the Thermite reaction
assessment: Matching activity: Ore type to extraction method
Fill in the blanks: Definitions of Gangue, Ore, and Mineral

lesson_plan.title: Refining and Corrosion Prevention
topics_covered: Refining of metals high in reactivity
teaching_strategy: Labeled diagram of electrolytic refining (Anode, Cathode, Electrolyte)
Explanation of 'Anode Mud'
Samples showing different prevention coatings
student_activities: Draw the electrolytic refining setup for Copper
List 3 alloys and their constituent metals
assessment: Short answer: How does galvanization protect iron?

lesson_plan.title: Revision and Assessment
teaching_strategy: Concept mapping on the board
Solving sample board questions (Medium difficulty)
Doubt clarification
assessment: Evaluation of logical flow in descriptive answers

teaching_aids: Videos on Thermite reaction and extraction
teacher_tip: At this level, emphasize the difference between 'Roasting' and 'Calcination' using the mnemonic: **C**alcination is for **C**arbonates (heating in limited air), while Roasting is for Sulphides (heating in excess air).
